q38b:
A new draft Management Directive (MD) has been created to replace the existing Section 508 policy, which is currently distributed across several documents. This draft also addresses policy requirements from the Office of Management and Budget memorandum M-24-08. The MD is currently under stakeholder review.

Further enhancements and expansions have been made to the resources available on the internal Section 508 guidance website. This includes expanded information on authoring tools, developer and testing tools, software development platforms, recommended testing methodologies, supplementary accessibility-related standards, and understanding the needs of people with disabilities. Numerous guidance notes have been added to help interpret the requirements in the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, version 2.2.

Forms for software and hardware intake requests have been updated to request that an accessibility conformance report be attached when available.

Guidance on the agency’s Technical Reference Model (TRM) website has been updated to emphasize the need for market research and for considering Section 508 requirements when selecting an IT product to meet business needs.

Two major new agency-wide Blanket Purchase Agreement (BPA) contracts for IT infrastructure and operational support, as well as IT development services, were solicited with strong Section 508 requirements. Two related draft BPA Call Performance Work Statements, to date, have incorporated Section 508 requirements into service areas. These contracts will provide a foundation for robust contractor support for accessibility in future IT services.

The tailorable Section 508 requirements language for use in the agency’s Statement of Work and Performance Work Statement templates has been updated to reflect lessons learned and current Federal policy.

Updates have been made to the Section 508 Exception and Exemption Documentation for Acquisitions of ICT Products and Services template, making it easier to understand and complete.

An additional web accessibility testing tool, IBM Equal Access Accessibility Checker, has been made available to agency employees.

Default settings have been changed to ensure that the Accessibility Checker tool in Microsoft Word, Excel, and PowerPoint runs automatically for all users and cannot be turned off.

A Statement of Work was prepared for the acquisition of technical support services for the Section 508 program. However, solicitation has been delayed until fiscal year 2026 due to completing funding priorities.
q38c: Over the past year, the NRC Section 508 program made meaningful progress in advancing accessibility objectives, as reflected in the accomplishments noted in response to question 38b. At the same time, resource constraints limited the pace of improvement, and some compliance gaps remain. While a request for additional staff support through a rotational assignment could not be fulfilled due to competing mission priorities, the program continues to identify opportunities to strengthen accessibility and remains committed to advancing compliance across the agency.
q38d:
The Section 508 program is optimistic that additional resources will become available in fiscal year 2026.

Questions 3c and 3d: There is no central shared service for Section 508 Conformance Testing and Section 508 ICT Remediation. These activities are conducted at the program and contract level.

Test results for the top 10 viewed internal intranet web pages: There were 10 top-viewed internal intranet web pages available to test, but comprehensive manual conformance testing was not performed due to lack of in-scope contracted resources.

q38b:
Enhanced Metrics
We introduced new metrics to better track and analyze accessibility across our technology portfolio. By leveraging real-time data, our testing team can quickly identify and address project team challenges without disrupting design and development. This data-driven approach allows us to proactively support our customers and has made our Section 508 Program more effective at preventing and reducing defects, enabling us to shift resources to new and emerging priorities. The impact of these changes is reflected in our follow-up procedures and outcomes.
Follow-up Procedures
We improved our follow-up process with application teams by using dashboards connected to our defect database, allowing us to track teams and defects throughout their lifecycle—similar to a Customer Relationship Management system (CRM). Our centralized, supportive service enables project teams to regularly and iteratively test their applications, with many teams now testing more than once a month. As a result, report writing time has been cut in half, total defects found have decreased by over 24% year-over-year, and defect resolution time has been reduced by two-thirds.
Improved Testing Tools
We enhanced the usability of our test tracking tools, providing customers with clearer data and step-by-step instructions. Because we both support project teams and design our own enterprise tools, we have a deep understanding of culture, process, and how to best provide accessibility support throughout the lifecycle. The teams we support now regularly check their status, and our tools guide them through the process based on their testing outcomes.
q38c: Our most significant and persistent challenge in increasing Section 508 compliance is supporting the IT system needs of employees with disabilities while meeting federal security and regulatory requirements. Security protocols and other regulations continue to limit our ability to provide the flexibility and system performance needed for assistive technologies. While the team who is responsible for distributing and supporting assistive technology consistently adapts and implements incremental improvements, the core challenge remains unchanged. We continue to do what we can within existing constraints, but balancing robust security and privacy protections with accessibility is a fundamental and ongoing difficulty that cannot be fully resolved under current conditions. Therefore, we do not have any requests that would benefit these challenges.
q38d:
SSA’s Section 508 Program is a centralized, agency-wide initiative within the Office of the Chief Information Officer (CIO), led by full-time Program Manager Pierce Crowell since 2016. As of May 31, 2025, the program is staffed by 18 FTEs and 6 contractors, delivering a range of services including application and web content testing, procurement assistance, document remediation, and the collection and application of user feedback. While the Section 508 Program focuses on improving the accessibility and quality of agency IT, other SSA groups are responsible for accommodations, assistive technology support, and complaint resolution.
Recognizing market limitations, SSA applies legal exceptions to IT purchases when necessary. During the reporting period, one acquisition qualified under the Fundamental Alteration Exception, and 709 purchases fell under the Best Meets Exception.
Accessibility awareness is high across SSA, particularly among content creators. Rather than relying solely on formalized and mandatory training, the Section 508 Program emphasizes real-world, experiential learning tailored to each project team’s content. This approach has resulted in a significant and sustained reduction in overall defects (more than 25% annually). Formal training for professional roles is provided upon request (developer, contracts, designers, analysts web masters, etc.), but our primary focus is on offering teams repeatable, practical learning experiences using their own content, at a time that works best for them. In short, teams learn accessibility by having our experts show them how to fix their own defects.
Our testing operation is grounded in risk management principles to prioritize work, maximize efficacy, and increase return on investment. Our manual web and software testing operation is robust: of the 699 test reports published to 260 project teams, only 10 applications have outstanding defects today. After our project teams fixed 433 out of 480 defects found this period (a 90% fix rate), 96% of the applications we tested are now bug-free. This success is driven by detailed, context-specific fixing instructions provided in our test reports. Over the past three years, the number of issues found has dropped by more than 25% annually, even as testing volume remains steady.
Several questions request a total estimate of ICT within specific IT categories (e.g., hardware, software). However, we use accurate methods for tracking IT accessibility progress and do not attempt to estimate assets at such a broad aggregate level. For these questions, we provide a list of the IT items we have tested within each requested category. Additionally, counting IT by page is not feasible for us, as most of our manual testing is conducted on highly dynamic single-page applications. While our web crawler provides page counts (6,325 pages scanned), the 79 public-facing applications and 167 internal applications we tested likely represent thousands of potential pages.
We understand the intent behind measuring compliance and testing activity as a percentage within each category. However, this approach presents challenges, as definitions of a single ICT asset can vary and may not accurately reflect our environment. Instead, we rely on robust metrics derived from actual test data, which provide a more precise picture of our accessibility progress. 
We have established processes to ensure the procurement of accessible devices, conducted user studies on hardware accessibility, and have a dedicated group that supports the assistive technology needs of individuals with disabilities. This group continually tests AT compatibility and provides hardware alternatives. When designing hardware solutions, such as our check-in kiosks, we engage users with disabilities early in the process, well before any development begins. Our focus is on meeting individualized needs, and the market often requires us to seek alternatives and adaptive technologies. While hardware accessibility is very important, we first assess whether there is a tangible benefit and whether it addresses an actual risk.
During the reporting period, we also conducted two separate user studies on kiosks. We recruited several members of the public who are blind or have low vision and brought them into our usability labs to study three new features for our fleet of check-in kiosks.
At SSA, accessibility is seen as a driver of IT innovation and quality, not just a legal requirement—it is a requirement for IT success. While we face challenges, including sustaining many accessible legacy systems, we are also excited about new technologies and their potential to better serve users with disabilities. We continue to apply professional, market-proven methods to improve the accessibility of SSA technology, contribute to the Federal Accessibility Community, and look forward to this year’s assessment report.

q38b: Most changes affecting Section 508 compliance were implemented 1.5 years ago when the website was redesigned. See further description below.
q38c: ACUS is a micro agency with no components and fewer than a dozen FTEs. The agency enters contracts for HR and EEO services (with GSA), IT services (with an external vendor), and has no independent leasing authority (GSA does that, too). Our miniscule budget does not allow for rigorous or systematic testing (in-house or via contract) or dedicated Section 508 staff. The few software products employed at ACUS are generally off-the-shelf (since ACUS does not develop or operate its own software or mobile apps) and are commonly used throughout government (e.g., Microsoft products). Any service providers (e.g., website developers) are from the GSA schedule of approved contractors that meet government requirements. Imposing testing and similar requirements (even through a shared service provider) would be unduly burdensome at ACUS. Any testing is performed on an ad hoc basis, with priority given to congressionally mandated documents and website development activities.
q38d:
FYI, when ACUS redesigned its website approximately 1.5 years ago (because support for its web content management system was set to expire), ACUS worked with the external developers to ensure accessibility and to create the best user experience possible--minimal graphics and pictures, low visual clutter, no flashing images, appropriate contrast, plain English, etc. In addition, an accessibility checker was added for use by ACUS website content creators. The redesign and migration imposed a significant cost on the agency that caused ACUS to delay other important expenditures.

With regard to question 22 on ICT procurement, ACUS merely purchases licenses for off-the-shelf ICT--not the type of procurement contemplated by the question. Moreover, all such purchases fall below the micro-purchase threshold (and simplified acquisition threshold).

With regard to question 36 and the number of 508 complaints received, ACUS has NEVER received a 508 complaint--not in the last year, and not since the agency opened in 2010.

q38b: The Department has hired an experienced Section 508 Team Lead to serve as the Section 508 Program Manager. The Section 508 Program Office has been realigned from the Branch of Quality Management to the Division of IT Governance. This realignment positions the 508 Program for success. The Department conducted extensive (several times per week) role-based technical training from June 2024 until January 2025.
q38c:
. The Section 508 Program was reduced from 7 FTEs and 5 contractors to 4 FTEs and zero contractors. The loss in capacity affects DOL’s ability to deliver and scale training and testing activities. Among the most helpful things that GSA’s 508 Program could offer:
•        A license for a capable, relatively inexpensive automatic testing tool for web content (such as PopeTech) to track and store testing results.
•        A license for “training-in-a-box,” such as Deque University.
•        Tools and processes to execute manual testing that are not as resource-intensive as the Trusted Tester methodology. 
•        On-demand micro-learnings (short videos and articles) for high impact web accessibility fixes, including:
o        Accessible form labels
o        Accessible error messaging
o        Accessible accordions
o        Document structure including headings, regions and lists
o        Accessible page titles
o        Proper keyboard interaction and accessible naming when, for example, a clickable graphic is next to a text link and both elements go to the same place
•        A tool that visualizes tab stops, such as Accessibility Insights for Web (Accessibility Insights for Web is not suitable for DOL)
•        More guidance that avoiding PDF is a huge win for document accessibility! 
•        A tool to track Section 508 exceptions (and the provision of Alternative Means plans for Best Meets, Fundamental Alteration and Undue Burden) across government. It would be compelling to know, for instance “DOL used Best Meets 10,000 times in FY27, but a similar-sized agency used it 4,000 times. And here’s the evidence supplied to support the Best Meets determination.”)
•        Regular updates to Section508.gov that contain technical, practical tips for creating Section 508 compliant content.
•        Best practices (and pitfalls to avoid) for using Artificial Intelligence in creating, managing and evaluating Section 508 compliant content.
q38d: No

We began working with our  web team to require congressional reports to go through the remediation process prior to transmitting them to the Hill.  We have also begun requiring several department reports (Military Finance and MD-715) to be Section 508-compliant on an annual basis.
q38c:
Vacancies and the federal hiring freeze have had a serious impact on the Section 508 program.  Our single federal FTE position with full-time responsibility for Section 508 issues has been vacant all year.  

Answering the "total (documents, hardware, etc.) owned and operated" assessment questions is extremely difficult for a large agency with a literally global presence.  This data frequently doesn't exist even at lower levels, and when it can be roughly estimated at lower levels, collecting it is itself an onerous process.  

Additional Section 508 Resources, such as federal or contractor FTE Section 508 staff spread across the Department, would be extremely helpful, as would a reliable global inventory of all Department ICT.
q38d:
The amount of guesswork required to make "total owned or operated" estimates is especially annoying given that the subsequent questions use these extremely unreliable estimates as the foundation for evaluations of the health of our program (e.g. we have tested only a small fraction of our public-facing documents.)  I also feel that the "public-facing documents" question is itself somewhat unfair to agencies that have a vast number of historical or archival documents. 
We encountered a potential issue with questions 19b and 20b. When we entered a value of 0 and clicked "Save Draft," the values were not being saved. However, when we entered a value of 1, the system saved the data correctly. It appears that these fields may not be accepting 0 as a valid input.

q38b: Over the past year, VA’s Office of Section 508 Compliance has made progress in strengthening accessibility across digital acquisitions – progress made possible by the Office’s inclusion in the Federal Information Technology Acquisition (FITARA) process, which had not occurred in previous years. Despite limited resources due to staffing changes and ongoing realignment at VA, the Office now plays a critical role in identifying accessibility concerns, reviewing acquisition packages, and issuing formal letters of concern to vendors when products do not meet compliance standards. The FITARA Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) now includes Section 508 compliance as a formal requirement, and the Section 508 status and recommendation are included as a standing component of the weekly FITARA meetings. In FY25 to date, the Office has participated in 71 FITARA reviews, reviewed 309 contracts containing 159 ICT products, and recommended 30 contracts for letters of concern.  These ongoing efforts are intended to improve vendor accountability and reinforce VA’s commitment to equitable digital access for all users. Additionally, the Office aligned the Section 508 Defect Tracking Tool (DTT) with the VA Inventory System (VASI) to validate and maintain current accessibility status across systems and implemented a public-facing website that displays audit results and documented exceptions to promote transparency and accountability.
q38c: Over the past year, the Department has faced significant challenges in maintaining Section 508 compliance due to the loss of eight full-time employees, reducing the team to 15 and creating a fivefold workload increase. This staffing shortage has led to delays in more than 1,238 project reviews, a backlog of over 8,500 unresolved accessibility defects, unstaffed document reviews (800 per year), and only one SME supporting eLearning reviews (750 per year). The reduced capacity has also impacted the ability to provide sufficient Section 508 training and conduct critical outreach, while inaccessible ICT tools are being deployed without adequate compliance checks.
q38d:
The Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) is advancing toward a fully centralized Section 508 program, with the goal of eliminating decentralized accessibility functions across staff offices. By consolidating all 508 compliance activities under a single, enterprise-managed authority, VA is ensuring consistent application of accessibility standards, reducing duplication of effort, and enhancing accountability and efficiency in meeting federal accessibility requirements. This centralized Section 508 program also enables the provision of standardized training and resources across the entire organization. By centralizing these efforts, the VA ensures that all personnel involved in the development and maintenance of digital services are well-informed and knowledgeable about the latest accessibility requirements and standards.
The Office of Section 508 Compliance also continues to navigate significant challenges surrounding resistance from major vendors such as Microsoft to fully meet accessibility requirements, as well as reluctance from some internal senior stakeholders to adopt necessary contract language changes, efforts that, in some cases, appear aimed at circumventing Section 508 legal requirements. To overcome these challenges and ensure accessible digital services for Veterans, increased and funded staff is urgently needed.

q38b:
The EPA rolled out a process to integrate Section 508 compliance into the Authority to Operate (ATO) process. An ATO is a formal declaration by the CIO that accepts responsibility for the operations of a system and officially declares that the system is authorized to operate. An ATO is required for all Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA) systems or applications operating within the EPA enterprise environment. By integrating Section 508 into the ATO process, EPA has a control gate to increase the agency's rate of 508 compliance. 

EPA has also integrated an Accessibility Compliance Report (ACR) into our new software request process. EPA added a requirement for the collection of 508 documentation to the early stages of the Information and Communication Technology (ICT) acquisition lifecycle.   

Through these two processes, EPA is integrating 508 into the software and system lifecycle.
q38c:
EPA has deployed a tool for automated 508 compliance testing of public websites. This has not been applied to the intranet due to security concerns with scanning internal resources with an external tool.  

Recommend GSA provide a tool to federal agencies for automated 508 scanning of internal websites that is also compliant with federal security standards.

q38b: FERC has implemented additional Section 508 awareness training courses and expanded support by proactively engaging users to provide assistance with ensuring their communication products are accessible. The Section 508 Compliance Training Plan is reviewed and revised each year to ensure the appropriate ICT roles have the knowledge and skills needed to ensure accessible ICT. We have continued to ensure every employee onboarded at FERC receives Introduction to Section 508 training to increase awareness. We have maintained a contract to Deque University to provide in-depth technical training for specific technical roles of both Federal and contractor staff in the CIO organization responsible for the management, design, testing, and implementation of ICT. We continue to evaluate agency policies for compliance with ICT requirements and continue to conduct periodic testing of new and legacy applications /software to identify non-compliant features and remediation requirements to add to team backlogs, resulting in an increase in compliance through early engagement, testing, and integration into the technology development lifecycle. FERC continues to leverage the Section 508 SME to provide coaching and remediation support to development teams as well as requests for assistance across the agency to ensure communications are accessible. We continue to look at ways to further the Section 508 Compliance Program, awareness, understanding, and activities for FERC.
q38c: The challenges FERC has faced in the past year include the need for additional resources as Section 508 requirements have increased due to federal mandates and more awareness across the agency. Many of the FERC applications are not public facing but some leverage legacy technologies. As we continue to modernize our applications to modern technology platforms, accessibility requirements are embedded, and the agency assesses a lower risk for nonconformance. With additional resources, the Section 508 Program will be able to provide more support and address other areas of need identified by this annual assessment such as more manual versus automated testing. Additionally, while majority of FERC solicitations are awarded from contract vehicles that include overarching Section 508 requirements e.g. GSA and NASA GWACs, the FERC Acquisition and Procurement team continues to identify and define guidance and processes to ensure Section 508 is integrated into the acquisition lifecycle.
q38d: FERC continues to invest in modernizing our internal and public facing applications as well as provide accessibility support services and training to Federal and contractor staff. FERC has prioritized accessibility by streamlining processes, proactively introducing accessibility considerations early in project planning and procurement, leveraging available resources, and ongoing accessibility testing of applications, software, and communication products. The FERC Section 508 Compliance Program continues to mature, and we anticipate more integration into the functions and services of the Commission through policy, guidance, and technical requirements as we further collaborations between IT, communications, and program teams. FERC strives to be a model employer of persons with disabilities and to provide employees, members of the public, and its constituents with disabilities with equally effective access to our programs and services.

q38b: From FY 2024 to the present (FY 2025), the Federal Mine Safety and Health Review Commission (FMSHRC) has advanced Section 508 compliance by designating a Section 508 Program Manager, publishing a Notice of Rights on accessibility of technology and facilities, updating Section 504 and 508 complaint procedures, and improving the accessibility of official publications such as the Blue Books, which are now published as searchable PDFs with hyperlinked tables of contents and metadata tagging for easier retrieval. The Commission has also integrated accessibility requirements into procurement and IT modernization projects to ensure compliance is built into acquisitions, and enrolled its public webpage into the Federal Digital Experience program to analyze visitor data and improve usability. Looking forward, FMSHRC will obtain the necessary resources for testing and remediation processes to ensure WCAG 2.1 Level AA conformance, provide the Section 508 Program Manager with the resources necessary to implement program requirements and monitor compliance, train staff and contractors on accessible content creation, enhance metadata tagging of web-based publications, and strengthen transparency through reporting consistent with OMB Memorandum M-24-08 and the Government-wide Section 508 Assessment process.
q38c:
Over the past year, the Federal Mine Safety and Health Review Commission (FMSHRC) has faced several challenges related to Section 508 compliance, primarily due to its status as a small, independent agency with limited staffing and resources. Key challenges include:

Resource constraints: With a small staff, there is only one subject matter expert (SME) available who also serves in multiple roles, leaving limited capacity to dedicate full-time effort to accessibility testing, document remediation, and digital compliance.

Legacy documents and systems: Large volumes of historical case records, orders, and publications remain in formats that are not fully accessible, requiring significant time and technical expertise to remediate.

Limited in-house expertise: Staff members often balance accessibility responsibilities with other program duties, which can result in delays or uneven application of Section 508 standards.

To overcome these challenges, the following would be most helpful:

Additional funding to support accessibility testing, remediation, and training.

Access to government-wide tools and resources, such as automated testing platforms and centralized remediation services.

Targeted training for staff and contractors on accessible content creation, while also providing direct support for the Section 508 Program Manager to implement program requirements.

Dedicated support or coordination from GSA or OMB to help small agencies like FMSHRC meet Section 508 and digital experience mandates without duplicating efforts already underway at larger agencies.
q38d:
As a small agency, the Federal Mine Safety and Health Review Commission (FMSHRC) faces unique challenges in sustaining Section 508 compliance. Automated accessibility tools are helpful but catch only part of the issues. Manual testing is essential to ensure full compliance; however, the Commission currently relies on its Section 508 Program Manager to manually screen all items, which is both time-consuming and burdensome given the Program Manager also holds multiple roles within the agency.
To address this gap, it would be extremely helpful if government-wide support were available for small agencies, such as a shared service or centralized resource to conduct manual testing and provide expert guidance. This would reduce the burden on individual staff, improve consistency of accessibility reviews, and enable small agencies like FMSHRC to meet compliance obligations more effectively while still managing other critical mission responsibilities.

q38c: The NTSB has taken several concrete steps to improve Section 508 compliance.  We acquired Acquia Optimize, a digital quality assurance tool that supports ongoing monitoring and enhancement of website accessibility. We contracted an external vendor to conduct a comprehensive audit of our public website, resulting in a detailed baseline report on accessibility. Using this report, we have begun remediating identified issues as resources permit. Additionally, we implemented formal website posting guidance requiring all electronic documents to undergo Section 508 compliance review prior to publication.
q38d:
Entering the testing, remediation, and conformance answers for each category was difficult. In the spreadsheet, these items were grouped together by category (e.g., Hardware, Software, Electronic Documents, Web Pages, and Internal Pages), but in the online form, they were presented separately without any clear instructions or notice of the change. This forced us to constantly switch back and forth between the form and the spreadsheet, which was time-consuming and confusing.

Additionally, when certain questions were not applicable to our organization and were skipped, the numbering in the form shifted. As a result, it became hard to track which responses corresponded to which questions. The final numbering in the system does not match our original data entry spreadsheet, making it difficult to verify accuracy and completeness. Also, using the same terminology between the spreadsheet and the on-line form would be beneficial, i.e. web content vs. public facing web pages, no mention of internal web pages in the spreadsheet vs. online form.

q38b:
The changes the agency made this year to increase Section 508 compliance was to do the following: 
-        Increase the promotion of personalized group training – vs the broader training that was provided monthly – that focuses on up to 18 different topic areas - for groups of employees in similar departments, divisions or teams to understand Section 508 law and its impact on *their* information communication technology (ICT) content that they create.  When the focus was on their content, participants could more easily ask targeted questions because the end result included an actual “official work product” that would be ready for their intended audience.
-        The Section 508 team proactively partnered with the procurement department to create a contract focused on the prioritized remediation of key acquisition documents that would be available to agency’s acquisition community stakeholders (i.e., CORs, CO) and their customers.
-        The Section 508 Team leveraged a contract to help reorganize the agency’s Section 508 Library of resources available via their internal SharePoint site to make self-service capabilities of finding, sorting and retrieving resources available to agency employees.
-        The Section 508 team more aggressively promoted the showcasing of their Accessibility Tools User Group with various department leaders, who then encouraged their teams to attend, the meetings focused on sharing how the accessibility tools available within the agency could enhance “baking in accessibility” into their projects as forethought instead of an afterthought.
All of these activities has shifted the culture for many stakeholders in the agency to shift from where we have to “ask to collaborate” with them to where they “invite us to collaborate” with them.
q38c:
The challenges the agency faced last year in regards to the Section 508 compliance has been consistently focused on mandatory training being provided to agency employees still includes accessibility issues in the training itself and the alternative resources provided that are suppose to overcome the initial accessibility issues. 

Some of the elements in place to overcome these challenges is ensuring key stakeholders have access (licenses) to the accessibility tools available to ensure the deliverable is reviewed prior to being deployed agency wide

Additional plans to address to challenge include coordinating with key stakeholders involved to ensure content deliverables received either externally or internally related to mandatory training and the alternative resources made available have a completed checklist documenting their testing *prior* to the deployment to agency employees.

q38b:
Over the past year, we have implemented several key improvements to strengthen our Section 508 program and embed accessibility into agency processes.

We are now fully integrated into the ICT intake process, ensuring accessibility considerations are addressed early in the technology lifecycle. We developed, published, and maintain a variety of learning resources to guide staff in remediation and testing, supporting both introductory and role-based training needs.

We reviewed agency policies for inclusion of 508 requirements and initiated consultations with business areas on targeted policy edits to institutionalize accessibility expectations. Additionally, we partnered with developers to ensure accessibility conformance is systematically built into development workflows for the public website, internal SharePoint environments, and PowerApps solutions.

These actions have increased our ability to proactively manage compliance, improve staff capability in accessibility practices, and ensure accessibility is embedded into the design, development, and maintenance of key agency ICT platforms.
q38c:
Our small accessibility team manages Section 508 compliance across procurement, policy, training, resource creation, remediation, and ICT testing. Limited staffing makes full lifecycle integration challenging.

We recommend:

Centralized Federal 508 Repository – A FedRAMP-style program for accessibility would provide pre-validated conformance documentation for ICT products, streamlining procurement and reducing duplicative agency testing .

Improved Platform Guidance – Federal-level, detailed resources for PowerApps, mobile apps, and SharePoint, since vendor accessibility documentation is insufficient .

Shared Interagency Resource Hub – A government-wide portal where agencies can share training, checklists, test scripts, and remediation workflows, avoiding redundant development.

These measures would strengthen reach, promote consistent compliance, and ensure federal employees and the public with disabilities have equal access to information and services.
q38d:
While this year’s Government-wide Section 508 Assessment was shorter and easier to complete—about half the questions of prior years—it excluded many elements that reflect a mature accessibility program. The removal of these topics could create the false impression that those areas are less important to meeting federal accessibility requirements.

Historically, required reporting on diverse aspects of a comprehensive 508 program (for example, inclusion of accessibility in employee performance plans) has been a valuable lever for 508 program leads to advance such initiatives within their agencies. A mandated, graded reporting requirement carries far more influence than a general suggestion or a non‑evaluated survey prompt.

By eliminating these previously scored elements, the new format limits our ability to demonstrate year‑over‑year progress on key program components in the formal annual report. This change creates concern that our overall rating may decline—not due to decreased conformance, but because important improvement areas are no longer recognized in the official scoring—even though our agency has achieved significant advancements in accessibility policy, processes, and conformance outcomes.
